🌍 Weather Trend Forecasting

Global Weather Repository · Basic Assessment · AI Engineering Intern

📊 EDA 🧹 Data Cleaning 🤖 3 ML Models 📈 Time Series 🌡️ 15 Cities · 3 Years
Dataset Overview
📁
16,440
Total Records
🏙️
15
Cities Worldwide
🌐
14
Countries
📋
27+
Features
📅
3 yrs
2022 – 2024
1 · Data Cleaning & Preprocessing
1

Missing Value Imputation

~2% of values were missing across temperature, humidity, precipitation, and wind_kph. Applied city-level median imputation to preserve local climate characteristics rather than using global mean.

2

Outlier Removal (IQR Method)

Applied 1st–99th percentile IQR filtering on temperature, humidity, wind speed, and precipitation. This removed physically implausible readings while keeping extreme-but-real climate events.

3

Normalization

Humidity normalized to [0, 1] scale (÷100). UV index normalized relative to dataset maximum. Ensures consistent scale for downstream modeling.

4

Feature Engineering

Extracted: month, day_of_year, year from the last_updated timestamp. Mapped month to season (Spring/Summer/Autumn/Winter), accounting for Southern Hemisphere cities.

2 · Exploratory Data Analysis
Uncovering patterns, correlations, and trends across 15 global cities.

🌡️ Temperature & Humidity Distribution

Temperature ranges vary widely across continents. Asia shows bimodal distribution due to mix of tropical and temperate cities.

📅 Monthly Temperature Trend by Continent

Clear seasonal cycles visible. Oceania (Southern Hemisphere) shows inverted seasonality vs. Northern Hemisphere continents.

🌧️ Average Monthly Precipitation Heatmap (City × Month)

Mumbai shows strong monsoon signal (Jun–Sep). Dubai and Cairo remain consistently dry. Singapore and São Paulo have high year-round precipitation.

🔗 Feature Correlation Matrix

UV index and temperature show strong positive correlation. Cloud cover negatively correlates with UV and visibility.

🍂 Seasonal Temperature & Precipitation Patterns

Summer shows highest median temperature and widest IQR. Winter precipitation is more variable due to snow vs rain differences across cities.

🏆 Coldest vs Hottest Cities (Average Temperature)

Moscow records the lowest average temperature; Dubai and Singapore dominate the warm end. Large latitude-driven spread across the dataset.

📌 Temperature ↔ Humidity: r ≈ –0.30 ☀️ UV ↔ Temperature: r ≈ +0.45 ☁️ Cloud cover reduces visibility (r ≈ –0.40) 🌧️ Mumbai monsoon peak: 8–12mm avg/day in July ❄️ Moscow seasonal swing: ~35°C difference summer vs winter
3 · Forecasting Models
Target: Daily average temperature for New York City · Train: 80% (876d) · Test: 20% (220d)
①

Linear Regression + Fourier Seasonality

Uses a time trend plus two harmonics (sin/cos at annual and semi-annual frequencies) to model the periodic temperature cycle. Simple, interpretable baseline.

②

Holt-Winters Exponential Smoothing

Additive trend + additive seasonality (period = 365 days). Parameters optimized via MLE. Handles level, trend, and seasonal shifts adaptively.

③

Random Forest (Lag Features)

Uses the last 14 days of temperature as input features. 150 estimators, max depth 8. Captures non-linear autocorrelation patterns.

ModelMAE (°C)RMSE (°C)R²Notes
Linear Regression~15.2~16.8~–2.1Underfits — simple but captures trend direction
Holt-Winters~3.2~4.2~0.81Good seasonal fit; slight lag at turning points
✅ Random Forest~2.7~3.5~0.87Best overall — captures non-linear lag dependencies

📊 Forecast vs Actual — Test Period

Random Forest closely tracks actual temperature dynamics. Holt-Winters performs well but smooths sharp transitions. Linear Regression struggles with non-linear seasonality.

📏 Model Evaluation Metrics

MAE, RMSE, and R² comparison across all three models. Random Forest dominates on all metrics.

🔮 30-Day Ahead Forecast (Random Forest)

Recursive multi-step forecast using the best model. Predicts the next 30 days of daily average temperature for New York City.

4 · Key Insights & Conclusions
💡

Seasonality dominates temperature variance

Over 70% of temperature variation is explained by seasonal cycles. Lag-based models that implicitly learn these cycles outperform explicit trend models.

💡

City-level imputation is critical

Global median imputation would introduce cross-climate noise. Using city-level medians preserves local signal, especially for extreme climates like Dubai and Moscow.

💡

Random Forest outperforms classic time-series methods

With just 14-day lag windows, RF achieves R²=0.87 — better than Holt-Winters (0.81) and far better than Linear Regression (–2.1). Non-linear interactions between recent days matter.

💡

Precipitation is harder to forecast than temperature

Precipitation follows a heavy-tailed distribution (many near-zero days, rare extreme events). A dedicated model (e.g., zero-inflated regression) would be needed for accurate precip forecasting.
